title: Philemon: Gospel Reconciliation
objective: See how the gospel transforms social relationships through forgiveness, honor, and restored fellowship.

required_passages.text: I appeal to you for my child, Onesimus... receive him no longer as a bondservant but more than a bondservant, as a beloved brother... refresh my heart in Christ.

segments.audio_script: Philemon is short but profound: gospel theology embodied in relational reconciliation.

Listen to Philemon. Paul writes from prison on behalf of Onesimus, once useless but now useful, asking Philemon to receive him as a beloved brother. Paul offers to cover any debt and appeals on the basis of love rather than command, modeling Christlike mediation and reconciliation.
In a world shaped by status and coercion, Paul reframes relationship in terms of family in Christ.
The gospel does not remain abstract; it rewires power, debt, and identity. Forgiveness and mutual honor become tangible signs of new creation community.
Key themes include reconciliation, forgiveness, mediation, brotherhood in Christ, and transformed relationships.

Review question: What does Paul ask Philemon to do for Onesimus in the letter to Philemon? Answer: to receive Onesimus back no longer as a slave but as a dear brother in the Lord — and to charge any debt he owes to Paul.
segments.correct_answer: receive Onesimus as a dear brother, no longer just as a slave — and charge his debt to Paul
acceptable_variants: paul asks philemon to welcome onesimus as a brother and credit any debt to paul
receive him as you would me — and charge anything he owes to my account
philemon is asked to see onesimus through the lens of the gospel as a beloved brother

segments.audio_script: Pauline Epistles conclude with reconciliation lived out. Next we can begin General Epistles and Revelation.

vocabulary.definition: The slave whose return to Philemon is the subject of Paul's letter; his name means useful in Greek — Paul puns on this: formerly useless but now useful, as a brother in Christ

Philemon's central question: does the gospel change how we relate across social divides? Paul does not abolish slavery institutionally but reframes the Philemon-Onesimus relationship around brotherhood in Christ
